data.title: Royal Bank of Canada Earnings Preview: Everything Looks Great, But I'm Not Buying
data.description:
Introduction

Royal Bank of Canada (NYSE:RY) (TSX:RY:CA) (RBC) is scheduled to report its Q2 2025 earnings results pre-market on May 29 before the market opens. After strong deliveries in the first quarter, these earnings will attract a lot of attention from investors. The stock of the company has been performing great lately, especially for a big bank. It looks like the market is starting to believe that the worst is behind Canadian banks, but for me, the conditions still feel fragile. Economic uncertainty is still in the air, and questions around credit quality and margins are still worrying investors.

Q1 2025 Recap

In Q1 2025, RY crushed expectations. The bank reported adjusted net income of C$5.25 billion, up 29% year-over-year, and adjusted EPS of C$3.62. Return on equity (ROE) reached 17.2%, which is very high for a bank this size, and it means that the company makes more than $0.17 on every dollar you, as a shareholder, put in.

Revenue came in at $11.59B, which was up 24% YoY, and beat the analyst expectations by an impressive $657 million. Net interest income alone jumped 26%, showing just how much they benefited from higher interest rates and strong volume growth in both deposits and loans.

Canadian Banking had a strong quarter, especially in Personal Banking, where NIM hit 2.50% and deposits grew 8% YoY (ex-HSBC Canada acquisition).

Capital Markets also showed impressive numbers, delivering record revenue of C$3.8 billion, up 27% YoY, driven by strong equity trading, FX, and loan syndication activity.

Wealth Management was another strong segment, with net income of about C$1 billion and 23% growth in client assets. Even City National Bank, its U.S. division, which has been under pressure lately, managed to report decent net interest income thanks to deposit growth and better cost control.

What’s expected for Q2?

Analysts expect RY to report EPS of around $2.28 and revenue of approximately $11.26 billion. That would be slightly lower than in the first quarter, which makes sense given seasonality, but still up compared to last year. The question is whether the bank can keep surprising to the upside, especially this quarter, with all of the tariffs and in general, the shaky macro conditions Canada has experienced.

One area investors will watch closely is credit. In Q1, RBC reported total provisions for credit losses (PCL) of C$1.05 billion, up significantly from the previous quarter. Most of the increase came from impaired loans, which rose for almost all segments, especially in Personal and Commercial Banking. If this trend continues, it could start to negatively impact earnings momentum.

HSBC Canada integration

RY reported on how its recent acquisition of HSBC Canada is performing, and the numbers already look good. In Q1, the bank said it had achieved about 70% of its C$740 million cost synergy target, and the run-rate savings were already over C$500 million. Management also reported C$1.7 billion in cumulative adjusted pre-provision, pre-tax earnings from HSBC Canada since the deal closed.

Investors will definitely be looking for an update on how the integration is going in Q2 as well, especially in terms of operational efficiencies and any signs of a lower number of clients (which so far hasn’t been a major issue).

Dividends

RY declared a C$1.48/share dividend in March, payable on May 23. That puts the yield at around 3.3%, which is decent given how much the stock has grown. The bank’s CET1 ratio is strong at 13.2%, and its liquidity coverage ratio is 128%, well above regulatory minimums. There’s also plenty of room for continued buybacks, with 1.9 million shares repurchased in Q1.

Macro and other risks

While the numbers look great, not everything seems to be positive. Earlier this year, RBC CEO Dave McKay spoke about how tariffs and geopolitical tensions could threaten global supply chains and weigh on business sentiment. Although some of the proposed tariffs have since been paused, the uncertainty is still there.

Another thing to pay attention to is the fact that RBC recently dropped its sustainable finance goals, citing regulatory constraints in Canada. This probably won’t hurt earnings right away, but it does hurt their image when it comes to being environmentally and socially responsible, which might matter to institutional investors.

Conclusion

Expectations for this quarter are very high, but the picture isn’t fully clear. Analysts are split—we have 4 upward revisions and 4 downward ones, which tells me that no one really understands what to expect. The company is solid, but Canada did get hit by tariffs this quarter, and it’s hard to say how much that might show up in the numbers.

Personally, I think the company looks great. It’s stable, well-run, and clearly has momentum. If I had to choose long-term, I’d probably lean toward calling Royal Bank of Canada a buy. But right now, ahead of earnings, I just don’t feel fully comfortable calling it a buy. There’s too much uncertainty. So for now, I’d rate it a hold, and wait to see what the numbers tell us.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Is Barclays A 'Buy' Following Its Q1 2025 Earnings?
data.description:
Barclays Investments Thesis

As I’ve covered in a previous article, I was not much bullish on Barclays (NYSE:BCS) despite its relatively cheap valuation some months ago, as the bank’s business mix is still significantly geared to cyclical segments, such as credit cards or corporate and investment banking. Despite that view, the European banking sector has performed quite well in recent months, as investors have rewarded most banks to focus on returning capital to shareholders, of which Barclays is no exception, and this mostly explains its strong run in recent months. Indeed, as shown in the next graph, its shares have returned more than 40%, including dividends, over the past few months, beating the overall stock market by a wide margin during the same period.

This strong share price performance was also supported by a positive operating performance in recent quarters. Indeed, the bank has released a couple of weeks ago its Q1 2025 earnings, which maintained a strong growth path across the group.

Its total revenues amounted to £7.7 billion, representing an increase of 11% YoY, supported by higher income from retail & corporate banking as favorable deposit volumes and the structural hedge led to increasing net interest income. Over the last quarter, about 68% of the bank’s revenue was generated by more stable operations, being on the right track to have more than 70% of its revenues coming from recurring activities by 2026.

This is important because one of Barclays' main weaknesses is its relatively large exposure to cyclical operating segments, which remains a key reason for a discounted valuation compared to peers. The bank has been changing the business mix toward more stable operations, namely in retail & corporate banking, both organically and through acquisitions. For instance, Barclays bought Tesco Bank in 2024 for £600 million, which was another step to increase its exposure to retail banking in the U.K. and have a larger share of revenues coming from core retail banking operations.

A strong support for increased revenues in the first quarter of 2025 was net interest income (NII), which increased by 13% YoY to £3 billion in the quarter. However, on a quarterly basis, its NII was flat, as higher deposit balances were offset by declining interest rates, as the Bank of England has cut its key rate several times over the past few months. This is a headwind for NII growth ahead, but despite that, the bank has made some efforts to reduce its sensitivity to rates, namely by reinvesting securities in its trading book at longer maturities, helping to smooth the impact of lower rates on its NII. Due to these efforts and a favorable deposit and volumes mix in recent months, Barclays raised its group NII guidance for the full year to more than £12.5 billion (vs. around £12.2 billion previously), a positive outcome showing that its NII is expected to be more resilient than previously thought during a period of declining interest rates.

In other segments, Barclays also reported a good performance in Investment Banking, supported by strong activity in markets, namely in fixed-income and credit, a trend that was also visible for its competitors, which, generally speaking, had a very good Q1 in trading activities. On the other hand, higher uncertainty due to the U.S. trade war has put some transactions on hold related to M&A, debt, or equity new issues, being negative for fee growth in some parts of Investment Banking.

Regarding its U.S. consumer business, this segment reported weaker operating trends, as it was the only segment that did not report revenue growth during Q1 2025, and U.S. card delinquency has increased in recent quarters, putting pressure on its profitability in this segment. Indeed, its return on tangible equity (RoTE) ratio was only 4.5% over the last quarter in the U.S. consumer business, being a very low level of profitability and a drag on the group’s overall profitability. The bank has been managing risk in this segment and doesn’t expect delinquency rates to rise much over the coming quarters, but this remains a potential risk source and something that investors should continue to monitor in the near term.

Regarding its costs, Barclays has been able to achieve some savings in recent quarters as part of its efficiency improvement program, aiming to achieve a lower cost-to-income ratio in the near future and also support a higher profitability ahead. During Q1 2025, its cost savings amounted to about £150 million, being on the right track to achieve its target of £500 million of cost savings during the full year. Despite these savings, its overall expenses increased by 4.5% YoY to more than £4.3 billion due to inflationary pressures, the integration of Tesco bank, and other costs. However, its cost growth was much lower than revenue growth in the quarter, allowing the bank to report positive leverage and a better efficiency ratio (57% in Q1 2025 vs. 60% one year ago).

Regarding its asset quality, Barclays continues to report acceptable levels of credit losses, given that its loan loss ratio was 61bps last quarter, representing a decline of 5bps from the previous quarter. While loan losses increased in the U.S. consumer business due to higher losses related to credit cards, this was offset by improving credit quality in the U.K. retail segment, showing that having a diversified business model helps to diversify risk and better manage potential areas of losses over the long term.

The bank has maintained its guidance of loan losses between 50-60bps throughout the cycle, but investors should consider that macroeconomic uncertainty has increased in recent months and the full impact of the U.S. trade war is still quite uncertain. Thus, the bank’s loan losses can be higher than currently expected and will be a slight headwind for earnings growth during the coming quarters.

Regarding its bottom-line, Barclays reported a net income of £1.86 billion in the last quarter, up by 20% YoY, boosted by its positive operating leverage, leading to a RoTE ratio of 14% (vs. 12.3% in Q1 2024). This level of profitability was also higher than its target for the full year (to be above 11%), showing that Barclays’ operating performance has been quite good, and if the bank can sustain its momentum over the coming quarters, it’s likely to report a higher RoTE than its own target during 2025.

On the capital front, Barclays’ CET1 ratio was 13.9% at the end of last March, a level that is at the top of its target range of 13-14% over the medium term. This means the bank has a solid capital position and does not need to retain many earnings in the near future, allowing it to distribute most earnings to shareholders through dividends and share buybacks. Indeed, its goal is to distribute at least £10 billion to shareholders between 2024-26, of which it already distributed £3 billion in 2024, leaving some room for higher distributions both in 2025 and 2026.

However, most of these capital returns should be made through share buybacks, as the bank prefers this type of return instead of dividends. Its dividend payout ratio is only about 30%, which is quite low compared to peers, and it’s not likely to change much in the near term. This explains why Barclays’ dividend yield is below-average compared to other European banks, as the bank is currently offering a yield of 2.6% while the average of the European banking sector is around 5.6%. Compared to its closest U.K. peers, such as HSBC Holdings (HSBC) or Lloyds Banking Group (LYG), its yield is also much lower, given that some of its peers offer yields around 5%, making its competitors better income plays than Barclays.

Regarding its valuation, Barclays is currently trading at around 0.76x book value, which is higher than compared to when I last covered it (0.6x book value at the time), but remains at a significant discount to other European banks (which trade on average at 1.18x book value) and also to its U.K. peers (average valuation of 0.97x book value). While there are still some issues that justify a discount to peers, such as its significant exposure to investment banking and U.S. credit cards, Barclays has successfully improved its fundamentals in recent years and is returning significant capital to shareholders, which are likely to remain a support for a higher share price ahead and potentially for a narrower valuation discount to peers in the future.

Conclusion

Barclays PLC remains one of the cheapest banks in Europe, and while I was cautious on its shares some months ago, I’m now switching my recommendation to ‘buy’ as the bank’s operating momentum remains good, and its rate sensitivity seems to be lower than previously expected. While there are some external risks that may affect its performance over the coming quarters, this doesn’t seem to be a major threat to its profitability, and its discounted valuation also provides some buffer for a possible economic slowdown ahead. This means its risk-reward profile is attractive for long-term investors, making Barclays an interesting value play in the European banking sector.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Applied Materials Q2 Earnings: Solid Print, But Missing Clear Catalysts
data.description:
Semicap names — and the semiconductor space more broadly — entered this quarter under the weight of renewed tariff and export control fears, something I’ve already discussed in my broader piece on trade restrictions. Peers like KLA Corporation (KLAC) and Lam Research Corporation (LRCX) delivered solid results, but their calls were largely overshadowed by geopolitical uncertainty rather than company-specific fundamentals.

That said, after a stretch of peak fear, the news flow has started to improve. Tensions with China seem to be de-escalating somewhat, and Saudi Arabia has emerged as a potential marginal buyer of AI chips — a development that, if it holds, could help extend the current AI cycle.

For Applied Materials, Inc. (NASDAQ:AMAT), China has been a consistent drag on revenue for some time, with U.S. export restrictions continuing to weigh. In CQ1 2025, China represented 32% of revenue, down from 43% a year earlier — a clear reflection of that pressure. In my view, China remains the single biggest risk for Semicaps. On the other hand, the ongoing U.S. reshoring effort and buildout of new fabs domestically should eventually become a structural tailwind.

So going into this quarter, the focus was twofold: first, how much of an impact tariffs might have on costs; and second, whether the company would revise its expectations for China and overall 2025 revenue given the evolving geopolitical landscape.

Applied Materials Q2 Earnings Review

Applied Materials' revenue rose 7% year over year to $7.1 billion, down 1% QoQ Non-GAAP gross margin rose 170 basis points year over year to 49.2%. Their guidance calls for 1% sequential revenue growth, to $7.2 billion, and 90 basis points of gross margin compression.

China revenue declined during the quarter, as expected — a result of both the ongoing trade restrictions and the pull-forward effect from last year, when Chinese customers rushed orders ahead of anticipated U.S. controls. China now accounts for 25% of total revenue, down sharply from previous peaks.

The impact of this drop was offset by strong demand from TSMC (TSM) and memory players.

DRAM strength was expected, largely due to HBM demand tied to AI applications — but that didn’t materialize. DRAM revenues actually declined on a year-over-year basis, which is concerning given how central this segment is to the current growth narrative. It raises a fair question: has the DRAM-HBM equipment cycle already peaked for AMAT?

So that has put DRAM in total, including HBM equipment, really continuing to operate at very high levels. So at least on our side, I wouldn't say cyclical low. I'd actually say maybe last year was a record year, this year will be close to a record year or it could be a record.

NAND is showing signs of life after a prolonged slump — a somewhat surprising move considering fab utilization remains low at players like Samsung and Kioxia/WD. This rebound seems less about end-demand and more about technology transitions. As CFO Brice Hill put it:

But yes, I think for that business, agree that utilization is lower, but most of those investments are made to upgrade process technologies and upgrade factories to the latest nodes.

AMAT also didn’t see a deterioration in demand due to tariffs, nor a big impact on costs — in part because inventory positions were built ahead of the restrictions. The cost headwinds are better reflected in the Q3 guidance and the lower gross margins presented.

All in all, the print was sustained by continued investments at the leading edge — especially around the transition to 2nm and gate-all-around — which helped offset slower growth in ICAPS. Similarly, NAND upgrades stepped in to counterbalance the year-over-year declines in DRAM. As management put it:

(...) investments at the leading edge more than offset declines for the ICAPS nodes that serve the IoT, Communications, Automotive, Power and Sensor markets; and growth in NAND upgrades more than offsetting year over-year declines in DRAM.

My Take On AMAT Stock

I was honestly surprised by both the NAND and DRAM aspects of this print. NAND strength stood out given low fab utilization at players like Samsung and Kioxia, suggesting that recent upside is more about tech transitions than true demand recovery. On the other hand, DRAM — which was expected to be a bright spot thanks to HBM and AI — actually declined YoY. That’s a red flag, and likely part of the reason the stock is selling off today. DRAM is supposed to be one of the main growth drivers going forward.

AMAT also came in a bit softer than peers KLAC and LRCX, both of which posted modest upside surprises. That relative underperformance probably added to investor concern.

The demand environment for 2025 WFE is still healthy, but growth is expected to be slow, and at this point, AMAT feels like it's lacking a clear catalyst. The sharp decline in China revenue is helping de-risk the exposure, but 25% is still a big chunk — and subject to further volatility.

ICAPS spending was down but came in better than I expected, which is notable given the softness most peers are seeing in their end markets. Overbuilding is always a risk in mature nodes, but at least there are signs of normalization in demand from players like TXN, STM, and others.

Geopolitics remain a major source of volatility. We're moving from Biden-era export controls — disruptive but at least well-defined — to the possibility of a new President Trump-era wave, which is far more unpredictable. The risk isn't just in what restrictions get implemented, but in how erratic the process could be. Even the talk of tariffs can shake Semicap sentiment, especially given Trump’s tendency to propose aggressive measures as a negotiation tactic.

In terms of business positioning, I tend to prefer KLAC and LRCX at this point. Lam’s exposure to memory is an advantage in this cycle, and metrology — where KLA leads — continues to gain share of capex in both memory and logic.

That said, AMAT does trade at a discount relative to peers, with lower valuation multiples.

Even with that in mind, I think the same caution I outlined for KLAC applies here as well. The geopolitical backdrop still poses a significant risk, especially given how much revenue AMAT still derives from China. And while valuation is more reasonable on a peer comparison basis, it doesn’t look particularly attractive relative to AMAT’s own historical levels.

For now, I prefer to stay on the sidelines.

data.title: Crypto Market Today: BTC-USD Consolidation And XRP-USD Potential Breakout
data.description:
By Zain Vawda

Bitcoin prices have entered another period of consolidation since Saturday, May 10, but bulls have been able to keep the price above the key psychological 100000 mark. Is the rally running out of steam or is more upside possible?

Firstly, we saw a similar period of consolidation around the 95000 handle before price exploded around $10,000 in a three-day span. This move higher followed about 11 days of consolidation. Markets usually see a more aggressive breakout the longer an asset consolidates. The question is, will bulls or bears prevail?

There are always conflicting views and, at times, signs which support both a bullish and bearish scenario. Let us take a look at what some of those factors might be moving forward.

Glassnode on-chain analytics

According to Glassnode, since hitting a low of $75k on April 9, Bitcoin has been on a strong upward trend, driven by spot market activity. Along the way, there have been periods of sideways trading, where prices stabilize before moving higher.

This "stair-step" pattern is visible in the Cost Basis Distribution (CBD) heatmap, which shows clusters of Bitcoin being bought at similar price levels over the past three months.

These accumulation phases happened before each upward move, leading to the most recent surge to $104k.

(Source: Glassnode)

Over the past 30 days, a key accumulation zone has formed between $93k and $95k. This range matches the cost basis of short-term holders - investors who joined the market in the last 155 days.

Because of this, this zone is expected to serve as strong support if the market experiences a short-term dip, as it’s a price range where investors are likely to find value and buy again.

Approaching The Highs

Bitcoin is now trading just below its all-time high of $109k, and excitement is building in the market. A great way to track this shift in sentiment is through the Short-Term Holder (STH) Supply in Profit/Loss Ratio, which shows how active investors are feeling.

This metric was especially useful during the April 7 correction, when it dropped to 0.03, meaning almost all short-term holders were at a loss. This happened as Bitcoin hit its $76k low. Since then, the ratio has jumped above 9.0, showing that over 90% of short-term holders are now in profit.

High values like this often signal riskier market conditions, as investors may start taking profits. While this can last for a while, it often leads to profit-taking or a local price peak if new demand slows down.

As long as the ratio stays well above 1.0, the bullish trend is likely to continue. But if it falls below 1.0 for an extended period, it could indicate weakening market strength and a potential trend reversal.

(Source: Glassnode)

Key Takeaways from Glassnode Data

Bitcoin’s climb back toward its all-time highs has been driven mainly by spot market activity, supported by strong buying on-chain and steady inflows off-chain. Most of the demand is coming from spot ETFs and major exchanges like Coinbase. A key support zone has formed around $95k, and with less selling pressure, the uptrend looks solid.

Meanwhile, the derivatives markets are lagging behind. Open interest and funding rates haven’t fully caught up with the spot market’s momentum. In the options market, traders seem cautiously optimistic, and there’s little sign of excessive long positions in the futures market right now.

Looking at crypto markets as a whole, there has been an interesting technical breakout on XRP.

Ripple (XRP-USD) Breakout? Can Bulls Push Price to Previous Highs?

Looking at Ripple (XRP-USD), whose popularity continues to grow, and there are two key macroeconomic factors supporting its rise. Potential ETF approvals, and the new US administration's approach to crypto which has further eased any SEC issues.

On Polymarket, traders now believe there’s a 79% chance that spot ETFs for XRP will get approved. Meanwhile, Ripple has faced setbacks from SEC disputes in recent years. However, with the SEC case now settled, Ripple is free to move forward with its plans, which could include a potential IPO.

Looking at XRP-USD from a technical standpoint, we had a triangle pattern in play which has been broken.

We have now seen a retest of the trendline following two successive days of bearish price action.

A triangle pattern break could lead to a significant upside move. For now though, the price needs to hold above the swing low at 2.30 for the bullish momentum to remain in play.

Ripple (XRP-USD) Daily Chart, May 16, 2025

(Source: TradingView.com)

Technical Analysis - BTC-USD

Bitcoin (BTC-USD), from a technical standpoint, has entered a period of consolidation similar to the one we witnessed around the 93000-95000 range a few weeks ago.

In that case, a bullish breakout followed, pushing the price beyond the 100000 mark. The move obviously coincided with improved market sentiment across the board.

The question is whether another bullish breakout can occur in the absence of a significant catalyst.

The RSI period-14 remains in overbought territory and may serve as a caution warning for bulls.

Immediate resistance may come into play around the 106000 handle before the all-time highs around 109356 comes into play.

Immediate support rests at 103647 before the 100000 mark comes into focus.

Bitcoin (BTC-USD) Daily Chart, May 16, 2025

(Source: TradingView.com)

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: VIG: A Core Dividend Growth ETF For Income Investors
data.description:
Vanguard Dividend Appreciation Index Fund: An essential piece of financial independence

The Vanguard Dividend Appreciation Index Fund ETF (NYSE: VIG) is a relatively straight forward dividend+growth mix ETF for an investor's core ETF positions. For those that run a core satellite strategy, anchored by roughly 60-70% index funds, and the rest single stock picks, or a pure ETF strategy, this fund serves to provide a below beta ride with near beta returns. This fund, unlike those focused on the S&P 500 aims to provide a larger part of it's total return by way of income than the more momentum and growth orientated ETFs like Vanguard's S&P 500 ETF (VOO) or State Street's S&P 500 ETF (SPY).

VIG, run by Vanguard, one of the largest mutual fund/ETF managers in the world, is also one of the lowest-cost to own. This is the Vanguard modus operandi with a weighted average expense ratio of only .07% across all of Vanguard's family of mutual funds and ETFs.

Where does VIG fit and who is it for

VIG is in the index family of funds concentrating on both dividends and the dividend growth rate. Dividend investing books will tell you that dividend growth is probably more important than absolute yield. This is an ETF that takes both of those elements equally into consideration.

High dividend yields are attractive, but that is ultimately a payout of the underlying company's earnings. That's less earnings to retain and less earnings to grow revenues and profits for those companies in the future. Total return is more of the focus in this fund than income alone. Appreciation + yield.

This fund is appropriate for all three stages of wealth: accumulation, preservation, and distribution. If purchased in the accumulation phase, it is probably more appropriate in the latter stages which is in the 40-50 year old age range onward. It preserves more growth aspects than a pure high yield fund while still avoiding the unconstrained market cap weighting of the S&P 500 or Nasdaq 100 index funds which do not rebalance or limit the percentage of constituents within the funds.

The index VIG follows

This is a passively run dividend ETF strategy. That means the managers take almost nothing from your compounding pie. The index that VIG follows was compiled by Standard and Poor's (S&P), the same company that compiles the S&P 500 index and the Dow 30 index. The index VIG replicates is called the S&P U.S. Dividend Growers Index.

Being passively run allows the fund to replicate this strategy algorithmically rather than being actively managed. With an expense ratio of only .05%, it's one of the lowest expense ratios you'll find for an index fund that replicates an index strategy outside of the S&P 500.

The index screen follows these basic rules:

Index Objective and Highlights The S&P Dividend Growers Index Series measures the performance of companies that have followed a policy of consistently increasing dividends every year for a specified number of years. The indices are subject to an indicated annual dividend yield exclusion, with the top 25% highest yield ranked eligible companies from the index universe excluded from index inclusion. Companies classified as part of the Global Industry Classification Standard [GICS] Real Estate Investment Trust Industry [REITs] are excluded. Constituents are float-adjusted market capitalization [FMC] weighted, subject to a single constituent weight cap of 4%.

  • Liquidity [median daily value traded] :S&P U.S. Dividend Growers: US$ 1 million (US$ 500,000 for current constituents)
  • Dividend Growth: 10 years of dividend growth [only based on the fixed portion of dividends, not special or variable]
  • Must be on one of the following US exchanges: NYSE,NTSE Arca, NYSE American,NASDAQ Global Select Market, NASDAQ Select Market, NASDAQ Capital Market, Cboe BZX, CBOE BYX, Cboe EDGA , Cboe EDGX

The logic in this index construction is to find the best companies with 10 years of dividend growth and to exclude the top 25% highest yielding companies from the index. This may seem counterintuitive for a dividend fund, but again, a larger payout from the start either means the stock has been crushed and lacks momentum, or they will have trouble growing their dividends in the future because they are not retaining enough earnings.

REITs are also excluded here as the fund only distributes qualified dividends that qualify for the lowest marginal tax rates. In fact, REITs are excluded from almost every dividend centric ETF you'll find as their inclusion would populate the screeners with a ton of REITs as they have to payout 90% of earnings in the form of a dividend by regulation and retain almost no cash. Most of their financing is through debt or share issuance. REITs should be looked at in a different category.

Observing long-term yield on cost

An important tool set that Seeking Alpha has is the ability to chart the long-term "yield on cost" for a stock or a fund. This is a visualization of what kind of dividend yield an investor gets on what they paid for a share at different intervals on a timeline. This is located on the "dividend yield" tab.

A good dividend stock or fund should have a curve that is up and to the right on the timeline. This indicates that holders are getting paid more and more as the dividend grows over the time the investor holds. Similar to buying a rental property and raising the rent every year. On top of that, dividends get preferential tax treatment for US holders, with dividends taxed at below marginal tax rates if qualified.

From Vanguard:

Ordinary dividends are taxed at the regular income tax rates, which are the same rates applied to your salary or wages. Qualified dividends are subject to a 0%, 15%, or 20% tax rate, depending on your level of taxable income.

It pays to buy-and-hold dividend funds that also have growth of dividends at the center of the equation as the dividend yield on your cost basis should look better and better over time.

Let's take a look at the yield on cost chart for VIG:

This is one of the smoother lines you'll see for a dividend growth fund. This fund is actually older than most other dividend growth ETFs you'll see in the market, having been established in 2006. It went through the GFC of 2008-2012 and buyers at the nadir of 2009 actually now get an 11.5% yield on cost. This is not typical, but a common result when buying dividend growth funds during a crash and holding on, this is one of their unique attributes, the goal of consistent dividend growth through thick and thin.

Here is a demonstration of some dividend growth scenarios if held for 10 and 20 year periods, adhering to common starting yields for the fund and annual dividend growth rates.

Scenario A- 10-year holding period

  • 1.8% yield
  • Dividend compound annual growth rate [CAGR] of 9.9%
  • Could grow to a 4.6% yield on cost at year 10

Scenario B- 20-year holding period

  • 1.8% yield
  • Dividend compound annual growth rate [CAGR] of 9.9%
  • Could grow to a 11.89% yield on cost at year 20

Thus in a hypothetical scenario, $10,000 at year one pays you $180 in dividends/year in the first year, $460/year by year 10 and $1,189/year by year 20. This does not take into consideration investors using dividend reinvestment programs [DRIP] rather than taking cash dividends, this would make the dividend payment per year even larger at all intervals by just having more shares.

An investor with a substantial amount to invest today and a 20-year time horizon for compounding to work, could have a nice quarterly income payment from such a fund in retirement by turning DRIP off and receiving cash, or selling shares that have both appreciated and accreted over this period. This is a great optionality to have in your golden years.

The VIG ETF Holdings

The top 10 holdings as I write this, are an excellent combination of all the essentials of what makes America run. VIG is a market-cap weighted index, so the selection list will have the largest companies by value of shares outstanding [market cap] right at the top of the list. This at current is Apple (AAPL) and Microsoft (MSFT) at a $2.97 trillion and $3.26 trillion market cap, respectively.

To make sure dividend yield, and not absolute market cap dominance has a balance, any holding in the fund is limited to 4% of the total weight and rebalanced quarterly. This should realign bigger dividend payers with those that retain more earnings but might be growing faster in market cap [the yield is also reduced as market cap grows for new ETF fund inflows].

This strategy is different from the S&P 500 and index funds that follow it, which do not cap any of its holdings at a certain percentage of the portfolio. The S&P 500 is a pure momentum strategy, and each holding is market-cap weighted and never rebalanced. VIG is a great blend of both worlds [growth and income] and limits volatility versus an S&P 500 market cap momentum strategy while not giving up much in total return.

This is well demonstrated in this 10-year backtest versus Vanguard's S&P 500 ETF (VOO). While the total return lags VOO a tad, the max drawdown was 300+ basis points less in the worst drawdown period and only about half of the negative performance in the worst year at -9.81% versus -18.19% for the S&P 500. This is what funds like this are for; they smooth out the ride and still get you near market performance with less stress.

That very volatility can be the element that prevents an investor from reaping the rewards of long-term compounding in buy-and-hold strategies. Being able to stick with equities somehow, some way, is the key to financial freedom and a secure retirement. VIG is a great fund to keep investors from deviating off the path of their equity strategy long term.

Example

Here is an example, a very long 20+ year view of VIG, AT&T (T), a short-term cash equivalent proxy in (BIL), and a diversified bond portfolio from Vanguard (BND). I chose AT&T because it has been derided as a poor investment over a long period, almost junk bond-like because of its high dividend yield historically and lack of appreciation. VIG handily beats both cash and bonds with a 388% total return through its existence. Bonds over 20+ years only returned 69.73%, and cash 22.9%. Even AT&T got in excess of 200% total return over this period.

The key in equity investing is patience. Unless we hit another 1981 scenario where the 10-year Treasury shoots up to 10%-15%, equities should remain the most important part for portfolios with longer than 10-year time horizons.

My ranking guide

This is the ranking profile I keep on my desktop and pinboard. VIG fits right in the middle of growth and value for index funds and is a good blend to get you a growing yield, while not giving up too much appreciation through lack of retained earnings for it's index constituents. At 341 holdings, it is also one of the most diversified dividend ETFs that exist.

In financial planning, there are three stages of wealth: accumulation, preservation, and distribution. VIG is appropriate for all phases but again, leaning more toward the later stages of accumulation. VIG does give up some return compared to funds #1 through #3, but VIG also helps keep you in the game.

This articles answers these three main questions about VIG:

  1. What is the main focus of VIG's strategy?
  2. How has VIG performed in up and down markets?
  3. Is VIG less volatile than the overall market?

Editor's note: This article is intended to provide a general overview of the ETF for education purposes only and, unlike other articles on Seeking Alpha, does not offer an investment opinion about the ETF.

data.title: Amazon: The Whole Package, Long-Term Buy For This Tough Grader (Upgrade)
data.description:
Kenio Fontes co-authored this article.

My family jokes about it all the time. I order something innocuous on Amazon.com, Inc. (NASDAQ:AMZN), and as soon as click the send button on my phone, someone says, "has it arrived yet?" This company, which was built over decades of patience, and which I've tracked since back when it was simply an online book store, is the best executor of its business plan that I can think of.

I am a self-admitted tough grader when it comes to stocks. My broad market view is that stocks should be considered rentals in most cases, owning to a secular high level of valuation, and a 15-year goosed up economic period driven by cheap money.

But AMZN is different, at least in that rare case like this one, where I am willing to close my eyes and look up in 5 years to see how I'm doing. OK, I could never do that with a stock, but I will say this: even if I collar it with options, beyond the modest current position I have outside of my main portfolio, I would probably keep that collar loose. That is, I'll go out further in time, I'll allow for more downside room, and I'll focus on where the stock could be in perhaps 2-3 years out. More on that at the end of this article.

AMZN: a long-term must-have for me

It is one of the companies that I consider a must-have in a long-term portfolio. Of course, strategically speaking, stock-picking isn't for everyone, and even for some, it's reasonable to follow strategies that don't have Amazon or other big tech in the portfolio. Nevertheless, whether through direct exposure, options, or ETF investing, Amazon is one of the companies that I consider to be very important in portfolio composition today, and ends up providing exposure to long-term fundamental trends.

Consider that even investors who only invest via ETFs have substantial exposure to Amazon, a factor that I find very attractive. The Dow Jones Industrial Average Index (DJI), for example, has around 3% of Amazon. The SPDR® S&P 500 ETF Trust (SPY), around 4%, and the Invesco QQQ Trust ETF (QQQ), 5.75%.

And just as investing in ETFs guarantees you diversification, Amazon also brings some of this to your thesis, increasingly surprising as the “everything store,” or more accurately, the everything company, with so many optionality that can surprise over the quarters.

Earnings pop in sync with broad stock market

My comments about Q1 can be seen here. Initially, the market interpreted this quarter's result as negative, mainly due to the guidance, which was below the midpoint, but in my view, the result was very positive and showed exactly what Amazon needs to deliver: a combination of consistent revenue growth and efficiency capture (margin expansion).

A few days later, the stock went up a lot, and this reading turned out to be more interesting. The guidance looked bad, but in the midst of such a chaotic scenario, it was reasonable. Now, with a clearer scenario, expectations may be even better for the short term. And of course, the indexing issue also ends up boosting the stock as people buy QQQ or SPY.

Although I don't believe that Amazon is a good investment at any price, it's very important to emphasize how much potential the thesis can unlock in the long term.

AWS is a value-generating machine that, as a leader in cloud, can continue to capture the growth of this market over the next 5-10 years, the potential for which could be a CAGR of up to 20% driven by trends in enterprise demand for AI, Big Data, and increasingly scalable and automated technologies. The capacity for expansion also seems far from over. Recently, Amazon announced an investment of $4 billion in Chile and $5 billion in Saudi Arabia for AI training and other AWS services.

The potential for generating value goes beyond core e-commerce and cloud, as Project Kuiper illustrates. Generating more than $100 billion in cash a year and with a healthy capital structure, Amazon can afford to announce billions of dollars in investments in this type of technology (even if it's not core), so that it can maintain accelerated growth and increasingly build a large and efficient ecosystem.

Even with some risks, such as competition and the sensitivity of the retail operation to macroeconomic scenarios and policies such as tariffs, Amazon is a very competitive behemoth with incredible long-term prospects, an interesting capital allocation track record, and room to continue growing in the coming years.

The Valuation? Not A Big Problem Right Now.

In 30 days, Amazon stock has risen 15%. Obviously, it would have been much more interesting to have bought the stock for $180, but still, at around $210, the company seems far from overvalued.

Multiples such as the price to operating cash flow are still at 20x, quite reasonable if you consider how the company could unlock value in the coming years (or decades). Even the forward price-to-earnings is at attractive levels, close to 33x.

33x may seem high, but we must remember that Amazon's depreciation is very high, and therefore its cash generation is substantially higher than earnings. Not only that, but with the top line growing close to double digit and a continuous margin expansion, in a few years, this multiple will become more attractive.

In other words, Amazon is a company that allows exposure to several secular trends at the same time, and has a unique ability to invest in new technologies and integrate them into its ecosystem to operate efficiently. And even with these unique and premium factors, its valuation is not that stretched, making it a compelling alternative for long-term investing.

Charting AMZN

Like the Nasdaq 100 (NDX) itself, the intermediate term outlook for AMZN indicates to me that just a bit more upward movement could unleash a very powerful move. However, $242 is the old high, so there's only about 15% until that likely showdown.

Removing the fancy technical markings, this below is an encouraging sign to me. AMZN is up only 23% the past 3 years. For a dynamic, iconic business like that, this is quite a slump.

This chart shows it even better. This is the total (not annualized) return of AMZN going back a very long way. 49% over 5 years for a top quality, wide moat business like this one might seem par for the course. But in AMZN's history, it has rarely been lower, and when it was, it historically marked a trough in the stock price.

Thoughts on collaring AMZN vs. holding it unhedged

I plan to finalize my decision as to how and when to collar AMZN in the near future, but I'm studying the pricing a bit more, and seeing if the stock and broad market may give me a chance to enter it a bit lower. I am planning to discuss with my investing group subscribers at our next weekly Monday live video session. But while I'm keeping the sanctity of that discussion reserved for them, here I can at least narrow it down a bit.

I am looking at buying the stock, selling calls against it and buying puts underneath it. An option "collar." The calls will expire further out than the puts, which is how I often do this. The premiums will offset, in that I'll receive for the calls about what I pay for the put protection, though that put protection will run out before the call option expires. So depending on how AMZN behaves over the coming year, price-wise, I could have to add more put protection down the road. But that would likely be if the stock is way up.

This is a bit cryptic, in part because collaring stocks is not at all an exact science. It could be, but even the most sophisticated ETFs that use option strategies tend to be active funds, not cookie-cutter index-based products that simply do the same thing over and over. Volatility has a huge impact on option prices, so the more one tries to make the same exact parameters work each time, the more the market could punish them for insisting on doing that.

The bottom line on AMZN for me is that I could just take a small position in the stock and leave well enough alone. But the collar approach gives me the ability to take a bigger position than I'd normally be comfortable with, since I know my loss is limited from day one.

Case in point: let's say the collar was set up so that the maximum downside risk was 10% from the purchase price of the stock. If I took a 5% position in AMZN in my portfolio, I'd be risking 10% x 5% = 0.5% of my total portfolio value, or $5,000 per $1 total portfolio size.

Now, if I bought a 5% AMZN position and the stock fell by 30% as it recently did, I'd have a paper loss of 1.5% of my portfolio, or $15,000 per $1mm total portfolio. Or, another way to look at it is that to match the risk level I'm taking in the collar described above, but only be willing to lose that same 30% on the stock held "unhedged," I could only take about a 1.7% position in AMZN. Because losing 30% on a 1.7% position is about the same $5,000 per $1mm portfolio that I risked with the sleep at night factor the collar provides.

data.title: Smooth Sailing Or Rocky Waters? How A Tariff Pause Could Impact Trade And Investing Outcome
data.description:
Global trade tensions have eased, but should investors expect rapid progress or a longer path? MoneyTalk discusses with Kevin Hebner, Managing Director and Global Investment Strategist, TD Epoch.

Transcript

Anthony Okolie: Well, after the shock of the so-called Liberation Day US trade announcement, we've seen a cooling of tensions, with Washington and Beijing dialing back tariffs. But should investors be preparing for a smooth sailing ahead or a rockier path? Joining us now to discuss is Kevin Hebner, global investment strategist with TD Epic.

And Kevin, thanks for joining us on the show.

Kevin Hebner: Very good to be here.

Anthony Okolie: All right. So, since we last had you on the program, we've had a truce of sorts with China and the United States on tariffs. But give us some context why Washington is right now trying to reshape its trade relationship with China.

Kevin Hebner: Yeah. So the Trump agenda from Inauguration Day until next November with the midterm elections and particularly the House elections, stage one is tariffs. And 95% were through tariffs. There's still some tariffs coming on the pharmaceutical sector. But to a large extent, that is done. And then we move on to trade deals. And so we now have the contours of trade deals with two countries, the UK and China.

And then we get tax cuts, the big, beautiful bill we were talking about earlier. That is a big deal. That's going to result, for example, in fiscal stimulus next year of about 0.8 percentage points of GDP. So it's a very big deal. And then the team can focus on deregulation, and energy, and finance, and tech, and construction, and a host of sectors. And then they can turn to weakening the US dollar. But in terms of the sequencing of his policy, we're moving from step one, tariffs, to step two, trade deals.

Anthony Okolie: OK. Now, technology obviously plays a crucial role in the trade relationship between the US and China. What impact is this on the trade story going forward?

Kevin Hebner: Yeah. So people think what's going on now is 90% about Trump and tariffs. It's really 90% about China and technology. So, US policies are responding to the rise of China challenging the US in three domains-- the economy, technology, and increasingly, defense capabilities. And so, with the rise of China, the US has to step back from a lot of their global roles, their hegemonic roles, the roles of empire, including being the global policeman. And I think this is one reason why you're getting the Iran deal.

Second be the US role of the consumer, as a consumer of last resort-- countries like China that have a lot of production, not very strong consumption, a place for them to dump their exports. And thirdly, the US stepping back from the role of the US dollar as the global reserve currency-- and the view from the White House is the special role for the dollar, it's meant the currency gets really strong, which makes it difficult to export and results in deindustrialization of the heartland United States, increased income inequality and a host of other imbalances.

Anthony Okolie: OK. So, given all these developments, how are you viewing these latest trade developments?

Kevin Hebner: Yeah. So, the joke on the Street is, it's the tackle trade. So Trump always chickens out. And so, with Liberation Day on April 2, we had extreme market action following that, and the pressure from bond yields, the dollar, equity markets, and a lot of CEOs and other politicians phoning Trump. He's ended up expediting some of the trade deals and, with the case of China, pretty much lowering tariffs on imports from China-- at least for 90 days-- dramatically, without China giving anything.

So, from the perspective of Beijing, it's been very good for them. They've just sat back. They've said nothing. They've compromised on nothing. And they've got tariffs down to roughly 30%.

Anthony Okolie: OK. So now, because of these restrictions, these tariffs, there's been potential shortages or expectations of shortages, particularly in the United States, around major shopping seasons like Christmas. Is this avoidable?

Kevin Hebner: Well, if the tariffs had stayed at 145%, then-- there is a real delay. Because the tariffs come into play once the goods are loaded on a ship, they cross the Pacific, they land in LA, they get on a truck, and they go across the country. So, we wouldn't really feel it until late August, early September, back-to-school time. And so there would have been a crunch then, and then there would have been a corresponding crunch as we got into the holiday shopping season.

But with tariffs at 30%, I think the economics look very different. And so you will see somewhat higher prices. And this is something that Walmart (WMT) commented on this morning, that they will have to raise some prices in response to the tariff increases. But I would think in terms of the shortages people feared-- and for the US, this is a supply shock in some sense akin to the COVID period.

And so, at 10%, I don't think there's a shock. 10% is the cost of doing business, in terms of tariffs. And that's likely what most countries will face. China is different. And as I was saying before, 90% of this is China, dealing with the imbalances between the US and Chinese economies. So, 30% strikes me as, in many sectors, a tariff rate that companies importing can deal with.

Anthony Okolie: OK. Now, who could be the winners and losers amid this shifting global picture?

Kevin Hebner: Yeah. So, the big winner from the period of hyperglobalization and free trade was China. And so, China now has this demand shock. About 3% of Chinese GDP comes from exports to the United States. Overall, exports represent about 30% of Chinese GDP. But a big chunk of that goes to the US. So that's a 3% demand shock if we do, in fact, have effectively an embargo on exports from China and the United States. So, I think China was the biggest winner, and they're likely the biggest loser.

There's other countries that have big current account surpluses. So that means they have weak domestic demand, excess capacity, and they're forced to sort of dump their exports, excess production into places like the United States. So, Japan, Korea, Taiwan, Germany-- places like this are the ones to lose.

There are also some US manufacturing companies-- well, multinational companies-- that have set up, really, quite complex supply chains across the globe. And those supply chains have to be refigured, either friendshored or homeshored. That will increase their cost. And some of them will take a bit of a hit as well-- and maybe Walmart is an example of that.

Anthony Okolie: OK, so let's talk about the markets. Because we've seen an impact on the markets from these tariffs. We've seen a bounceback from the drop in April. Where do you see markets going from here?

Kevin Hebner: So, going forward, you can come up with two scenarios. So, a pessimistic scenario is that Trump continues the way he was in early April-- so quite belligerent, treating his allies worse than he treats his adversaries. And so, America First means America alone. You get a chaotic breakdown in global trade. You get the rise of competing currency blocs. And you're essentially unwinding decades of globalization. The saying is, it takes a generation to grow a forest, it takes an hour to burn it down. So that's the pessimistic case.

I'm much more in the optimistic case. And as we were discussing before, we're just about finished tariffs. There'll be a couple more to come, particularly in pharmaceuticals. Then we move to trade deals. There's a lot of work to do that. And one real question is about capacity, and then tax cuts, which would be substantial deregulation, weakening the US dollar.

I think overall, this is good for equity markets broadly. I think it's very good for the tech sector because Trump is going to be extremely favorable for tech. And I also think it's good for some nonequity asset classes-- infrastructure-- given the need for ports, pipelines as we homeshore factories, I think infrastructure as an asset class looks terrific.

Original Post

data.title: thyssenkrupp: Incremental Utilisation Improvements Possible, But Challenged Outlook
data.description:
Thyssenkrupp's (OTCPK:TKAMY)(OTCPK:TYEKF) rise has been pretty understandable from a value perspective. Their marine systems business is picking up steam on the back of excellent defense outlook. The strong Thyssenkrupp balance sheet means that the residual value of the other businesses could be considered quite low, and with the Germans looking at their own debt brake and a more practical outlook in the EU being likely over the importance of supporting and ensconcing strategic European industry like steel, there is an appealing logic to Thyssenkrupp even at these run-up prices. On the other hand, the end markets are challenged and outlooks is worsening, and the tariff situation as well as the general plan to restructure the major steel business and reduce the scope of their operations are going to weigh on the business, with the hoped for savings likely to come in on a pretty protracted basis, taking a general view on how things tend to go for German restructurings with a tough structure of powerful trade unions and large employee bases. Steel and automotive technology are not sectors that we like at all right now. As for the decarbonisation, ESG is out of fashion now, and they are lucky at all to have been able to IPO Nucera at the value they did, with the post-IPO price performance of that business being terrible. The IPO was the subject of our coverage of Thyssenkrupp many years ago. Nonetheless, we acknowledge value here in Thyssenkrupp, and while we are negative on the end markets for the foreseeable future, there have been some exceptional hits to the H1 and Q2 figures that do mean a sequential improvement is probable for at least the steel business.

Latest Earnings

There are several key things from the earnings call that are worth keeping track of. Steel Europe segment is one that are looking to downsize a bit, targeting around 20% lower steel volume production in general over the course of this multiyear restructuring. They have gotten some investment from an outside investor in the business which remains a minority, but the company's plan is to receive more investment and make a joint venture out of the Steel Europe with EPCG. They are making an associated business plans for how the formation of this J.V might happen, to our understanding. Around 11k FTE redundancies are in some of the initial targets, which is a fair bit, but even that is going to be very tough to negotiate with the unions. This is them capitulating on the steel market, which is oversupplied, and where their European capacity cannot compete with other global players. We're going to note another unfortunate development which is while Europe might have had plans before to go protectionist on European steel, this could fall to the wayside in trade negotiations where the US consumer offtake of European products is going to be leveraged into less protectionism by Europe to maintain steady trade relations with the trade warriors in Trump administration.

In addition to that they have sold their electric steel business in India as a means to reduce their overall exposure to steel. A sale like this is generally convenient. They also are moving out of their J.V with HKM, where they have a supply agreement to provide HKM with products. They tried to find a buyer, since this is an ideal solution, but these talks have apparently collapsed. It seems that they will have to continue to handle the business' restructuring, which is going to be tiresome. In general, restructuring German businesses is difficult. The trade union agreements are tough to break out of, and real savings on the labour side can take years due to the labour protections. Similarly, in the automotive technology business, also struggling on account of weakening end market demand for consumer durables like cars, particularly with key offtake to markets like the US under threat from tariffs, they are targeting around 150 million EUR in cost savings, but these could take a while to materialise, more than it would in a US restructuring. They say run-rate in the next fiscal year but we would expect longer. It's not easy to fire people and they'll have to phase out facilities slowly. The negotiations are expected to conclude in summer, and from there it will take time to actually see the savings come in on the cost side, all while revenue is under threat as removes income from the topline effects. They have managed to cut 2600 FTE jobs (600 from business disposals), but that's a tiny proportion of the overall 95k employees FTE, and they are planning a very significant drop in their steel output so a lot of cuts still need to happen for the restructuring to be a success.

Sales achievement was the issue for the Q2, which showed a deceleration from the Q1 figures. Characterised by significant operating leverage, the operating line suffered.

Not every segment is bad. In AT, there are some positive aftermarket mix effects. Sales in AT have actually accelerated into the Q2. Moreover, the margin effects from the aftermarket contribution is accelerating segment margin, though YoY adjusted EBIT is weak. 10% tariffs are now in effect on this business in the Q3, in addition to other delivery frictions. While this has been passed onto customers it is a worsening of their terms of trade and weakens the competitiveness of their products to that significant US market.

Marine Systems has a strong order intake so far this year thanks to defense spending, sales are lifting and there is plenty of backlog to deliver - this is their submarine business. Though there is margin pressure here YoY, and a stable sequential development in adjusted EBIT.

Material services in the US also did well, accelerating into Q2 sequentially and resilient YoY. Margin significantly accelerated sequentially, though down YoY. This business is localised in the US - no direct tariff considerations. The decarbonisation technologies business is green hydrogen EPC solutions. We don't make much of this business.

Ultimately, the EBIT is down to almost zero on account of overall significant price and volume hits mainly in steel. The steel segment's declines nuked 50% of EBIT from the same quarter last year. The business is struggling in most of its end markets, though in Steel Europe some of it was one-off. There was a significant idling due to a major investment action in order to improve the plants in major facilities. Part of their production turned off for more than a quarter, so no sales on significant fixed costs. Utilisation is going to have to improve from dismal levels into the H2, which is positive, and explains the 250-500 mn EUR adjusted EBIT guidance for the steel segment as of now. Also, lower energy prices could give a hand to the economics of the business into H2 as well.

Bottom Line

Sequentially, things could actually be alright for Steel Europe thanks to idling now being over. But there is no question that the significant automotive markets across steel and the automotive technologies sector, as well as the general industrial outlook particularly in Europe, is dragging down on utilisation as well. With tariffs likely to worsen the outlook for European industry further, not helped by increased politicisation of trade relations with the US which could lead to less pragmatic outcomes as far as it concerns the trade negotiations on reciprocal tariffs, we are negative on automotive and on general industry, even German industry despite perhaps more leeway from the debt brake. The German economy is woefully overcapitalised due to the debt brake. Theoretically, the public sector could spend 3x what it's spending now and still have a solid balance sheet by European standards. Government spending is a big part of German GDP, so the prospects here are interesting, but it would depend on a suspension of the debt brake which is politically still unlikely.

While it could be encouraging to markets to see incremental improvement as utilisations rise into the H2, the bigger opportunity is with the proposed marine business spinoff (actually, a planned minority IPO pending shareholder approval). If you take a defense multiple for other marine levered defense businesses, crudely something like BAE Systems (OTCPK:BAESY), you get an 18x EV/EBIT multiple. That puts the marine business at a possible 2.23 billion EUR EV based on annualising H1 figures. Thyssenkrupp EV thanks to the healthy balance sheet is around 650 million EUR. It's clear to see that if this were to be the IPO price for the business, even if only a minority of that 2.23 is cashed in, the valuations for the other businesses are implied to be rather low. But, we cannot forget pensions. They are 5.7 billion EUR liability. They also have some hidden liabilities in the form of outstanding CAPEX requirements which we can't quantify, but are there and could be in the billions of growth/restructuring CAPEX. When taking this into account, even if in practical terms it's a very long-term liability, the appeal becomes lessened, but nonetheless remains as the 2.23 billion EV against around 6.3 billion EUR including pensions, means that the valuation of the marine business is around 35% of the EV despite being 5% of run-rate sales, and less than 30% of EBIT in a moment where the bigger steel business is under exceptional pressure, even idiosyncratic pressure on utilisation rates due to factory modernisations. The comprehensive Thyssenkrupp multiples are also a fraction of peers, despite a strong multiple being possible on the defense-levered businesses. There's something here, but we're not getting out of our chair for this.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: KDDI: Turning Positive On Strong Profit Growth And Improved Cash Management (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
My opinion of KDDI Corporation (OTCPK:KDDIY) (OTCPK:KDDIF) (9433.T) has become bullish.

The prior November 10, 2024 write-up was focused on KDDIF's investor-centric actions and 2QFY25 ("YE March 31, 2025") financials.

I'm raising KDDIY's rating from Hold to Buy. The company registered above-consensus FY2025 results. It also initiated favorable capital allocation changes.

Mid-Teens Operating Income Expansion Was A Pleasant Surprise

The Japanese ("JP") telecommunications ("telco") entity disclosed its latest fiscal year performance on Wednesday, May 14.

EBIT for KDDIY went up by 16% YoY ("Year-on-Year") to ¥1.12T for FY25. Analysts had anticipated a lower operating earnings of JPY 1,110M according to S&P Capital IQ data.

It's also encouraging that KDDIF has returned to positive growth territory. The group suffered from a -11% OP ("Operating Profit") drop in FY2024.

Competitive pressures in JP's mobile sector have eased. This is the main factor contributing to KDDIF's recent results beat and solid growth.

KDDIY's wireless ARPU ("Average Revenue Per User") increased +1% year-on-year for the quarter ended March 31, 2025. The same metric was down -0.5 percent and -0.3% in 3QFY25 and 2QFY25, respectively. This improvement didn't come at the expense of user attrition. Its subscriber base expanded by 20 bps ("basis points") YoY for both FY2025 and 4QFY25.

SBC ("SoftBank Corp") (OTCPK:SFBQF) (OTCPK:SOBKY) indicated at its May 8 earnings briefing that "it is a necessary time to revise prices". SOBKY is KDDIF's major rival. Its comments imply that the operating environment in the JP telco industry has gotten better. This explains why KDDIY is able to achieve ARPU increases in the recent financial period.

KDDIF operates in a high fixed-cost business. A modest rise in revenue ("ARPU") turns into substantial OP growth for the company.

Moving forward, I am optimistic about KDDI's prospects in the new year.

KDDIY plans to lift the prices of its unlimited and small/medium data packages for the core brand ("au") by around JPY 300 and ¥200, respectively in 3Q25 ("calendar year"). This supports the group's guidance that its FY26 earnings will surpass that for the previous year.

My prediction is that KDDI's EPS growth will accelerate from +12 percent in FY25 to 15% for FY2026. I have considered its recent outperformance and the proposed price hikes in the forecasts.

I'm Excited By Generous Distributions And Intention To Gear Up

The company has decided to share a bigger amount of cash with its stockholders. Also, investors might have fewer complaints about its "lazy" balance sheet in the future.

KDDIF has guided for a FY2026 DPS ("Dividend Per Share") of ¥80. This is equivalent to a reasonably high 40% payout and a one-tenth increase ("in absolute terms").

It also announced a fresh JPY 0.4T buyback program that will be in effect between mid-May and late-Dec this year ("calendar-year terms"). This is a good initiative for the company in two ways.

One thing to note is that KDDIY's key shareholders Toyota (TM) (OTCPK:TOYOF) and Kyocera (OTCPK:KYOCY) (OTCPK:KYOCF) have the intent to reduce their interests in non-core investments over time. The former's ambitious repurchase plan will allow it to acquire its own shares from TM and KYOCY directly. This helps to avoid a situation where KDDIF's stock is dumped in the open market.

Another point relates to KDDI's attractiveness in the eyes of income-focused investors. I estimate the stock's potential forward capital management yield ("cash dividends and buybacks divided by market cap") to be an enticing 7%. This takes into account both the group's cash distribution guide and the guided buybacks.

Separately, KDDIY mentioned at its FY2025 results call ("S&P Capital IQ transcript") that it is willing to take on increased borrowings.

The company's leverage ("net debt-to-EBITDA") ratio is low at 1.5 times. The same metric for SFBQF was 2.7x based on S&P Capital IQ data.

I think that KDDIF has been far too conservative in the past. It is under-leveraged as compared to other players in the telecom market.

KDDI is thinking of having its net debt-to-EBITDA go up to two times. This would provide the company with incremental capital of approximately ¥1,000 billion as per my calculations.

At the company's fiscal '25 briefing, KDDIY stressed that it wants to utilize "financial leverage" to "allocate more resources for growth".

Its results in FY26 and beyond are likely to exceed expectations. This assumes that additional capex ("capital expenditures") funded by debt translates to higher future profits.

Risks You Should Watch

I will become worried if KDDIY's churn rate spikes in response to pricier subscription plans.

KDDIF might also fail to impress the investing community in areas like cash distributions and financial management. An inability to complete the planned repurchases and a huge jump in gearing ("e.g. 3x or more") won't be well-received by the market.

My Final Remarks

I have upgraded KDDIY stock to a Buy.

The stock's potential upside is +33% in the event that its earnings multiple re-rates to a comparable level with its competitor. The TTM ("Trailing Twelve Months") P/E multiples for KDDI and SBC are 15x and 20x ("source: S&P Capital IQ") respectively.

The group's financial outlook and cash management have improved. My take is the valuation discount for KDDIF should gradually narrow and even close.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: UnitedHealth: Oh, It Can Get Worse (Sell Reiterated)
data.description:
This article is co-authored by Kenio Fontes

If you are thinking this is some sort of “victory lap” it is absolutely NOT that. It is using the latest 1-day tanking of UnitedHealth Group (NYSE:UNH) as a teachable moment, including updating our views on this former top-weighted Dow Jones Industrial component stock. UNH was about 10% of the Dow at one point.

As of Thursday's close, it was barely hanging on to the top 10 of that 30-stock index. And with the news surrounding the company now, with words like “fraud” being thrown around, I wonder if it will even be a DJIA member stock for very long.

Since our last analysis of UnitedHealth Group (UNH), the stock has fallen 45%, including the -11% on Thursday (the graphic immediately below does not include Thursday's session). A lot has changed since that, and this is one of the great examples of why a stock can continue to fall even with an already below-average valuation.

My late father was my first investment mentor. He taught me a lot about investing as a self-directed investor and trader. The first thing he taught me was that you never know how much a stock will fall. I enhanced that over the years to "any stock can go up in price at any time, for any reason." What matters is how much risk of major loss is attached to that pursuit of profits.

I use price charts as my guide, but I also lean on fundamental analysis and company quality. However, those are screeners to decide what I will be willing to own at some point. The "when to own it" part is predominantly chart-based. And while many in the audience have told me over and over that price-based analysis is somewhere between witchcraft and "totally worthless," I won't try to change minds. I'll just keep supplying cases in point where the charts told us this was a high-risk situation.

Yet, everyone wants to be 2 things, it seems:

  • A bottom buyer (a.k.a. buy the dips)
  • A hero

The rating thing continues to baffle me. Not that ratings exist, but that they are STILL taken as the gospel by so many investors. Oh, they work sometimes. But they don't work often enough to be given the level of blanket belief and reverence they still have. I am on record as frequently saying that ratings, mine included, only provide a sliver of the actual opinion. But since many don't tend to read the rationale, only the rating, they often miss the point. Investing can be simple, but the context is ALWAYS different for every investor.

So when I see the case of UNH ratings shown here, I simply shake my head. Nary a rating below hold, and nearly all are buys. And apparently, this has been the case since the stock was more than $600 a share. Where's the risk management here? How much negative performance on a stock is considered OK, even when it beats the market, which of course, is not the case here?

The takeaway for me is that UNH is symptomatic of a knee-jerk response to convince ourselves that a stock is cheap when its price falls a lot. I analyze stock prices for a living, and I say that is bunk. And valuation analysis on a short-term basis is right behind it in its ability to inform us in a way that helps us manage risk. The charts are the thing.

UNH: updated views on the stock

My feelings about the company remain nearly the same, even with these falls, but with the margin of safety having become even more attractive. One way of illustrating what I think of the company is the term "mixed feelings". It's a company with a business model that, in parts, is robust and has positive trends. But at the same time, there is a lot of complexity and parts of its business model that are debatable.

The most recent reason for the latest version of UNH's "how low can you go" is due to possible fraud, specifically in the Medicare program. As well as being uncertain and having the potential to affect the company's fundamentals, it also makes investors who are looking for reliable businesses with good governance and a business model that is not problematic shy away from the thesis.

The news of the fraud is not the only catalyst for these recent falls. A few days ago, the resignation of UNH's CEO was announced, due to "personal reasons", as well as the guidance for 2025 was suspended, while an increase in medical care activity and higher than expected medical costs were noticed.

The Valuation Got Better Amidst This Confusion

Due to this, the market has already adjusted expectations downwards, and now estimates an EPS of $23.69 for 2025, down from the previous range of around $26.

Even with this negative revision, the forward price-to-earnings looks very attractive. Currently, in after-hours trading, the company is trading around $280. With the $23.7 EPS, you have a price-to-earnings ratio of 11.8x for 2025. This equates to an earnings yield of almost 8.5%. For comparison’s sake, in the last analysis, the valuation grade was D-, now it's B-, at least prior to Thursday night's update.

But since confidence in these earnings is low, let's use the earnings yield for 2027. $33 would be the equivalent of an earnings yield close to 11.6%. Even with a relatively low payout of 30%, this would be enough to produce a dividend yield of 3.5% (i.e., with the potential to be much higher, like 7% if the company decides to distribute something closer to 60%).

For a company that has the scale and potential to overcome these (several and important) challenges over the next few years, this valuation seems pretty reasonable, right? Well, more or less. My take is that the current scenario is so unsettled, yet lacking real clarity, that the current discount seems right.

But it's also very important to bear in mind the upside risks. The first, and most obvious, would be a reversal of the bad sentiment hanging over the stock, but this would require a series of factors ranging from the sector's prospects to governance. The most likely thing for me would be the normalization of medical costs, and as the quarters go by, the company returns to milder results and outlooks without too many negative surprises, which could eventually lead to a re-rating of the multiple.

The Bottom Line

Here is what I wrote in the charting section of my last article, with UNH at $428 a share, versus $274 at Thursday's close.

Here's that same chart, updated. If anything, it looks worse.

If there's a bounce to occur, it would probably be in this area. But as noted, I'm not a buyer here, other than perhaps considering a "trial" position of maybe 1% of my portfolio, if that.

Last technical/price trend point to consider: this would not be the first major stock to fall all the way to its 2020 pandemic crash low. For UNH, that's around $188 a share, more than 30% down from here, still.

But if I were to "venture" into UNH to try to ride out a rebound, I would certainly do so using collars or some other similar hedge, given the unpredictable context.

Therefore, due to both the fundamental reasons and the technical reasons listed, I reiterate UNH's sell rating. Any stock can rally, but until this stock proves otherwise, it is still a falling knife, apart from the typical strong bounces along the way.
